title: The Simpsons 138Th Episode Spectacular

jokes.text: You may remember me from such Fox Network specials as... Alien NoseJob, and five fabulous weeks of The Chevy Chase Show.
jokes.setup: Troy's trademark introduction format building up to prestigious TV work
jokes.punchline: Two terrible examples - one absurd (Alien NoseJob) and one real TV failure (Chevy Chase Show)
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy for Troy McClure - the contrast between his pompous delivery and terrible credits. Real-world reference to Chevy Chase's failed show adds bite.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You may remember me from such films as 'Alien Nosejob' and the short-lived Chevy Chase Show.

jokes.text: America's favorite... non-prehistoric cartoon family
jokes.setup: Standard praise for America's favorite cartoon family
jokes.punchline: Oddly specific qualifier 'non-prehistoric' clearly targeting The Flintstones
jokes.explanation: Clever competitive dig at The Flintstones delivered as if it's a normal category. Good economy of language.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You may remember me from such films as 'Radioactive Man' and 'The Contrabulous Fabtraption of Professor Horatio Humperdink.' But today I'm here to talk about America's favorite non-prehistoric animated family — The Simpsons!

jokes.text: old favorites you can't see in syndication
jokes.setup: Promising viewers special content
jokes.punchline: Acknowledging censorship/editing of reruns
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-commentary on TV syndication practices, but delivered matter-of-factly without much comedic punch.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You may remember me from such DVDs as 'The Simpsons: Uncensored Collection' and 'The Simpsons: Director's Cut.' But I'm here to tell you that the episodes you're about to see have been heavily edited for content and time. Many scenes involving alcohol, violence, and adult language have been removed. Some characters' names have been changed. And several jokes that might offend have been replaced with safer alternatives. So sit back, relax, and enjoy The Simpsons — now with 40% less Simpsons!

jokes.text: D'oh!
jokes.setup: Classic Homer catchphrase in context
jokes.punchline: The familiar exclamation itself
jokes.explanation: Iconic catchphrase but no craft in this instance - just recognition value.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: the already-famous creator of such comics... as Damnation, Johnny Reb... and True Murder Stories
jokes.setup: Building up Matt Groening's impressive credentials
jokes.punchline: Completely fictional, dark comic titles that sound nothing like Life in Hell
jokes.explanation: Clever subversion of documentary format by inventing completely wrong but darkly funny comic titles for Groening.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You may remember me from such autobiographical comics as 'Life in Hell.' But you probably don't know about Matt Groening's other works, like 'Death in Suburbia,' 'Agony in the Afternoon,' and my personal favorite, 'Suffering: It's Not Just for Breakfast Anymore.'

jokes.text: The Tracy Ullman Show- the nation's showcase for psychiatrist jokes... and musical comedy numbers
jokes.setup: Describing Tracy Ullman Show as a prestigious platform
jokes.punchline: Reducing it to two very specific, dated comedy elements
jokes.explanation: Dry commentary on 80s variety show tropes, delivered with Troy's characteristic pompousness.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You may remember me from such prestigious television programs as The Tracey Ullman Show.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: Where I performed in a little sketch called 'The Simpsons.'

jokes.text: What is mind? No matter. What is matter? Never mind.
jokes.setup: Bart asking a deep philosophical question about consciousness
jokes.punchline: Homer delivers a classic wordplay response that sounds wise but is meaningless
jokes.explanation: Classic old joke perfectly delivered by Homer - shows his character's approach to parenting with false wisdom. Highly quotable despite being unoriginal.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, if a tree falls in the forest and no one's around to hear it, does it make a sound?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, Bart, that's like asking if a hot dog is a sandwich. The answer is yes... and no... and also pants.

jokes.text: Bedbugs?
jokes.setup: Marge's innocent 'don't let the bedbugs bite' saying
jokes.punchline: Bart's horrified realization that bedbugs might be real
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective child logic joke - taking a common saying literally creates genuine concern.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Don't let the bedbugs bite.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait, bedbugs are real?!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Of course they are, dear.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Why didn't anyone tell me this before?!

jokes.text: They haven't changed a bit, have they?
jokes.setup: Showing early Tracy Ullman shorts
jokes.punchline: Meta-commentary on how the characters are identical across decades
jokes.explanation: Self-aware joke about animated characters never aging or changing, breaking fourth wall cleverly.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You may remember me from such educational films as 'Firebat: Avenger of the Deep' and 'Pukahontas.' But you probably don't remember that these Tracy Ullman Show shorts are where The Simpsons began!

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: Notice how Homer, Marge, Bart, and Lisa looked exactly the same thirty years ago. Nothing's changed! Same design, same jokes, same eternal stagnation.

jokes.text: There's no perfect crime.
jokes.setup: Maggie seemingly getting away with eating cookies
jokes.punchline: Baby delivers profound moral lesson
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion - the baby who can't talk delivers the moral of the story. Perfect economy and character surprise.

dialogue.speaker: Maggie
dialogue.line: Sucking on pacifier while eating cookies

dialogue.speaker: Maggie
dialogue.line: Realizes she's been caught, looks at camera with knowing expression

dialogue.speaker: Maggie
dialogue.line: Removes pacifier and says: But I learned something today — if you're gonna do something wrong, just don't get caught.

jokes.text: And you'll be- Bartron, the evil robot from Mars gone berserk.
jokes.setup: Lisa assigning space patrol roles to family
jokes.punchline: Bart gets the over-the-top evil robot role
jokes.explanation: Good character work - Lisa casting Bart as the villain in her elaborate fantasy shows sibling dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Okay, everyone. I've assigned space patrol roles based on your personalities.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What's my job?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You're the ship's cook, Dad.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: And me?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You're the communications officer, Mom.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What about me?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You're the evil robot bent on destroying the ship and killing everyone aboard.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Excellent!

jokes.text: is a horrible, mutant boy.!
jokes.setup: Dramatic reveal of what's inside evil robot Bartron
jokes.punchline: It's just regular Bart described as 'horrible, mutant boy'
jokes.explanation: Perfect sibling insult disguised as sci-fi drama. Lisa's creative way of calling Bart horrible while maintaining the game.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: It's just regular Bart described as 'horrible, mutant boy'

jokes.text: Wake up, everybody! It's World War III!
jokes.setup: Homer waking everyone up urgently
jokes.punchline: Claiming it's World War III as a wake-up call
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer parenting - completely inappropriate method delivered with confidence. Shows his character's lack of boundaries.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wake up! Wake up, everyone! It's World War III!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, what are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm just trying to get you guys out of bed!

jokes.text: If this were really a nuclear war, we'd all be dead meat by now.
jokes.setup: Family in fallout shelter during fake emergency
jokes.punchline: Homer casually noting they'd already be dead
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered matter-of-factly. Homer's casual approach to nuclear annihilation is both funny and unsettling.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, if this were a real emergency, we'd already be dead.

jokes.text: Welcome to zee exotic world of undersea explorer... Bart Simpseau.
jokes.setup: Bart trying to make bath time more interesting
jokes.punchline: Creating elaborate French explorer persona in bathtub
jokes.explanation: Good child imagination comedy - Bart's elaborate fantasy to avoid admitting he's just taking a bath. The French accent adds character.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Sacré bleu! I am Jacques Cousteau, explorer of ze deep!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Zis bathtub, she is ze ocean, non? And zese rubber duckies, zey are my crew!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: We must descend into ze mysterious depths to discover ze secrets of ze sea!

jokes.text: The cash register says, 'N.R.A.4ever.' Just one of the hundreds of radical, right-wing messages... inserted into every show by creator Matt Groening.
jokes.setup: Pointing out background detail as political messaging
jokes.punchline: Claiming liberal Matt Groening inserts right-wing messages
jokes.explanation: Clever satire of conservative critics who see liberal bias everywhere. The irony is perfect - Groening is famously liberal.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You may remember me from such films as 'Radioactive Man' and 'The Simpsons.' Did you know that liberal Matt Groening secretly inserts right-wing propaganda into every episode?

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: Why, just last week, I spotted a subliminal message hidden in the background that said 'Buy more guns and cut taxes!'

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: It's the only explanation for why America keeps voting Republican!

jokes.text: That's not a question, professor.
jokes.setup: Reading viewer mail claiming to answer questions
jokes.punchline: Pointing out the 'question' is just an insult
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective meta-humor. Troy's literal interpretation of the format breaks the fourth wall nicely.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: Let's see here... 'Dear Troy, you're a huge jerk.' Well, that's not really a question, but I'll answer it anyway. No, I'm not.

jokes.text: Uh, what's your first name? - I don't know.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to pick up mail as Mr. Burns
jokes.punchline: Doesn't know Burns's first name despite working for him
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - attempts identity theft but hasn't thought it through. Shows his impulsiveness and lack of attention to detail.

dialogue.speaker: Postal Worker
dialogue.line: I need a signature from Mr. Burns.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's me, I'm Mr. Burns.

dialogue.speaker: Postal Worker
dialogue.line: What's your first name?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Uh... B?

jokes.text: I'm the magical man from Happy Land... in a gumdrop house on Lollipop Lane.
jokes.setup: Homer being sarcastic about making people happy
jokes.punchline: Increasingly ridiculous candy-themed fantasy location
jokes.explanation: Homer's sarcasm escalating into absurd candy fantasy. The escalation from 'Happy Land' to specific candy details is well-crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh sure, I'll just make everyone happy by taking them to a magical candy land where the trees are made of lollipops, the rivers flow with chocolate syrup, and the clouds rain gumdrops.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And then we can all skip through the gumdrop meadows, past the licorice forests, and swim in the marshmallow lakes!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why, we'll ride on candy canes down the caramel mountains and sleep in beds made of cotton candy!

jokes.text: Oh, by the way, I was being sarcastic. - Well, duh.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining his obvious sarcasm
jokes.punchline: Bart's dismissive response
jokes.explanation: Good father-son dynamic. Homer overexplaining obvious sarcasm and Bart's casual dismissal shows their relationship perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I was being sarcastic.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, nobody cares.

jokes.text: Hey, guys. Look what I smuggled aboard. - Homer, no! - They'll clog the instruments!
jokes.setup: Homer bringing something onto spacecraft
jokes.punchline: Bringing potato chips to space, causing instrument problems
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer prioritizing snacks over space mission safety. The specificity of 'clog the instruments' makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey, can I bring my potato chips on the spacecraft?

dialogue.speaker: Astronaut
dialogue.line: Sure, go ahead.

dialogue.speaker: Astronaut
dialogue.line: Oh no! The salt from the chips is corroding all our instruments!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Ants!
jokes.setup: Homer floating in space with chips
jokes.punchline: Single word reaction to ants in zero gravity
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - one word captures Homer's predicament. The visual of floating ants in space is inherently funny.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ants!

jokes.text: No TV and no beer make Homer something something.
jokes.setup: Homer's mental state deteriorating
jokes.punchline: Can't even finish the famous 'All work and no play' phrase
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback to The Shining with perfect Homer twist - too lazy/distracted to complete the iconic phrase. Became highly quotable meme.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: All work and no play makes Jack a dull... uh... dull what? I forget.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, who's Jack?

jokes.text: Go crazy? - Don't mind if I do!
jokes.setup: Bart suggesting Homer might go crazy
jokes.punchline: Homer treating insanity as a polite invitation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer response - treating a serious mental breakdown as a casual social invitation. The politeness makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you're going to go crazy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, thank you for inviting me!

jokes.text: Get outta my office!
jokes.setup: Trying to interview the show's creator
jokes.punchline: Groening immediately kicks them out
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective meta-joke. Groening refusing to participate in his own show's documentary is appropriately surreal.

dialogue.speaker: Matt Groening
dialogue.line: Get out of my office!

dialogue.speaker: Interviewer
dialogue.line: But we just wanted to ask you a few questions about—

dialogue.speaker: Matt Groening
dialogue.line: I said GET OUT!

jokes.text: If I came into your house and started sniffing at your crotch... and slobbering all over your face, what would you say? - Mmm. If you did it, sir?
jokes.setup: Burns describing annoying dog behavior
jokes.punchline: Smithers's reaction suggesting he'd enjoy it from Burns
jokes.explanation: Perfect Smithers moment - his barely concealed attraction to Burns creates uncomfortable comedy. The pause and 'sir' make it worse.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: That blasted dog won't stop barking at me. Keeps jumping up, licking my face, following me everywhere.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, with all due respect, that sounds... rather nice.

jokes.text: You're. Quite. Good. At. Turning. Me. On.
jokes.setup: Smithers working on computer
jokes.punchline: Computer voice with sexual double meaning
jokes.explanation: Simple pun but works well for Smithers character development. The robotic delivery makes the innuendo more awkward.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Oh, what a day. Time to unwind with my computer.

dialogue.speaker: Computer
dialogue.line: Good evening, Smithers. I'm ready to... process your requests.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Oh my, yes. I do hope you're adequately... equipped for this.

dialogue.speaker: Computer
dialogue.line: I assure you, my hard drive is fully... operational.

jokes.text: You know that dream where you're in bed, and they fly in through the window?
jokes.setup: Burns describing a recurring dream
jokes.punchline: Implying romantic dreams about flying figures
jokes.explanation: Adds to Burns's creepy character while remaining ambiguous. The casual way he shares this is appropriately uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I have this recurring dream where I'm on a great ship, and the water is made of semen.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: And these beautiful mermaids come up to the side of the boat, and they wave at me with the flimsiest of fins.

jokes.text: He's in his early 40s, is unmarried, and currently resides in Springfield.
jokes.setup: Viewers asking about 'the real deal' with Smithers
jokes.punchline: Completely bland, obvious biographical facts
jokes.explanation: Brilliant non-answer. After all the obvious gay subtext, Troy provides completely irrelevant demographic information. Perfect satire of corporate non-responses.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: Well, you may remember me from such educational films as 'Accepting An Incredible Bribe' and 'Kick Me.' But today, I'm here to set the record straight about Mr. Smithers. You see, viewers have been asking, 'Is Smithers the real deal?' Well, let me tell you some completely bland, obvious biographical facts. Smithers was born on a day. He has a first name. He wears clothes. And yes, he does exist. You may have seen him before. I'm Troy McClure, and you may remember me!

jokes.text: If you said 'Bleeding Gums' Murphy and Dr. Marvin Monroe... you are wrong- they were never popular.
jokes.setup: Setting up trivia question about disappeared characters
jokes.punchline: Admits they were just unpopular, not mysteriously absent
jokes.explanation: Brutally honest meta-commentary. Instead of explaining character disappearances with plot reasons, admits they just weren't working.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You may remember me from such episodes as... well, actually, I haven't been in many lately. But that's not because I mysteriously disappeared — I was just unpopular!

jokes.text: You've got some attitude, mister.
jokes.setup: Addressing viewer who thinks they've seen everything
jokes.punchline: Confrontational response to imaginary viewer challenge
jokes.explanation: Troy taking fictional viewer criticism personally. Good character moment but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You may remember me from such films as 'Dial M for Murderer' and 'Firecrackers 2: The Burning.'

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: But you haven't seen everything! I've got plenty more where that came from!

jokes.text: If you watch my show, I will send you this book featuring me... in a variety of sexually explicit positions.
jokes.setup: Krusty desperately trying to save his show
jokes.punchline: Offering inappropriate sexual material to children
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation of Krusty's desperation. Perfect character moment showing how far he'll go, completely inappropriate for children's TV.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Offering inappropriate sexual material to children

jokes.text: Hey! It's not really me. I used a stunt butt.
jokes.setup: Krusty realizing how bad his offer sounds
jokes.punchline: Making it worse by explaining the logistics
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - trying to make it better but making it so much worse. 'Stunt butt' is inherently funny and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, let me explain this better. You get paid in Krusty Bucks, which you can redeem at participating Krusty locations for merchandise or food items...

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: ...and by participating locations, I mean my office. And by food items, I mean the stuff I don't sell because nobody wants it.

jokes.text: Can I play 'Hemorrhoid Sufferer Number One'? - Ooh! Oh, that hurts! Aah! Oh, is there no relief?
jokes.setup: Krusty begging for work in infomercial
jokes.punchline: Enthusiastic audition for hemorrhoid commercial
jokes.explanation: Krusty's desperation leading to degrading commercial work. The immediate, over-the-top performance shows his professional dedication even in humiliation.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Please, I'm desperate! I'll do anything for work!

dialogue.speaker: Director
dialogue.line: Well, we're casting for a hemorrhoid commercial.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Hemorrhoids?! I'm your guy! I know those things intimately!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Do I get the part? Do I?!

jokes.text: Joker. You were supposed to take those out of the deck.
jokes.setup: Homer dealing cards in professional casino scene
jokes.punchline: Gives James Bond a joker, ruins the whole scene
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer incompetence - ruins a James Bond scene with basic dealer mistake. Shows how he'd fail at any job.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Here's your card, pal.

dialogue.speaker: James Bond
dialogue.line: A joker? This ruins everything.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? It's still a card.

jokes.text: 'Rules for Draw and Stud Poker?'
jokes.setup: Homer continuing to deal wrong cards
jokes.punchline: Gives him an instruction card instead of playing card
jokes.explanation: Escalates Homer's incompetence further. The specific absurdity of dealing instruction cards adds to the chaos.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Go fish.

dialogue.speaker: James Bond
dialogue.line: That's not a card, that's an instruction card.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, you gotta follow the instructions first.

jokes.text: Homer, please. You don't have to wolf down that 25-year-old candy... just to make me happy. - But it won't make you unhappy, right?
jokes.setup: Homer eating ancient candy to please his mother
jokes.punchline: More concerned about her feelings than food poisoning
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment showing Homer's people-pleasing nature. His twisted logic about happiness vs. health is perfectly Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Mona Simpson
dialogue.line: You eating that candy I brought you?

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Yeah, Mom, it's delicious.

dialogue.speaker: Mona Simpson
dialogue.line: Good, because I found it in my purse from 1985.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: 1985?! That's... that's over 30 years old!

dialogue.speaker: Mona Simpson
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: So nothing. It's great, Mom. Really great.

jokes.text: No, I work at the nuclear power plant. - Oh, Homer. - Well, you'll be happy to know I don't work very hard.
jokes.setup: Mother disappointed Homer doesn't work for NASA anymore
jokes.punchline: Homer thinks admitting laziness will cheer her up
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - thinks admitting he's a slacker will somehow be comforting. Shows his complete misunderstanding of what disappoints people.

dialogue.speaker: Homer's Mother
dialogue.line: I'm so disappointed you don't work for NASA anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, Mom, if it makes you feel any better, I'm lazy.

jokes.text: Actually, I'm bringing the plant down from the inside.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to reassure his mother about his work
jokes.punchline: Accidentally admits to sabotaging nuclear plant
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - Homer trying to sound impressive accidentally confesses to terrorism. The nuclear context makes it darker and funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't worry, Mom. I'm very careful at the nuclear plant. Safety is my number one priority.

dialogue.speaker: Mona
dialogue.line: That's good, Homie.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I follow all the rules. Well, except for that time I deliberately loosened the coolant valve to leave early on Fridays. And when I rigged the backup generator so it wouldn't work. And that incident with the uranium... but other than that, safety first!

jokes.text: I'd sell my soul for a Formula One racing car. - That can be arranged. - Changed my mind. Sorry.
jokes.setup: Bart making typical 'sell my soul' comment
jokes.punchline: Devil appears, Bart immediately backs out
jokes.explanation: Good subversion of the soul-selling trope. Bart's casual backing out shows his survival instincts despite his troublemaking nature.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'd sell my soul for a donut.

dialogue.speaker: Devil
dialogue.line: Done deal.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa! I was just talking!

jokes.text: Bart, stop pestering Satan!
jokes.setup: Marge dealing with family chaos
jokes.punchline: Treating Satan like any other person Bart shouldn't bother
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge line - treating the supernatural like everyday parenting. The casual tone about Satan is brilliantly absurd and very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I treat Satan like any other person Bart shouldn't bother.

jokes.text: But we did win. - That's okay. The box is empty.
jokes.setup: Hutz delivering free pizza despite winning case
jokes.punchline: Admits the pizza box is empty anyway
jokes.explanation: Typical Lionel Hutz incompetence - even his scam doesn't work. Shows his character as simultaneously sneaky and completely ineffectual.

dialogue.speaker: Lionel Hutz
dialogue.line: Yes, well, I may have won the case, but justice doesn't pay the bills. So I'm delivering free pizza to the community.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That's very generous of you, Mr. Hutz.

dialogue.speaker: Lionel Hutz
dialogue.line: Well, I should mention... the pizza box is empty.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Empty?

dialogue.speaker: Lionel Hutz
dialogue.line: I couldn't afford the pizza.

jokes.text: Their clothes are different from my clothes. Look at what they're wearin'.
jokes.setup: Apu trying to understand American comedy
jokes.punchline: Reducing all humor to superficial clothing differences
jokes.explanation: Good cultural outsider perspective. Apu's literal interpretation of visual comedy shows how humor doesn't always translate across cultures.

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: I have been studying American comedy, but I do not understand. You reduce all humor to superficial clothing differences?

jokes.text: Or what? You'll release the dogs, or the bees... or the dogs with bees in their mouth... and when they bark, they shoot bees at you?
jokes.setup: Homer challenging Mr. Burns's threats
jokes.punchline: Increasingly ridiculous combination of dogs and bees
jokes.explanation: One of the most quoted Simpsons lines ever. Perfect escalation from simple to absurdly specific. The visual of bee-shooting dogs is brilliantly ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You think you're tough, Mr. Burns? I'm not afraid of you!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Oh really? What if I unleashed my hounds upon you?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dogs? I laugh at dogs!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: What about dogs AND bees?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You don't scare me!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Dogs AND bees AND dynamite?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Still not scared!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Alright then... dogs AND bees AND dynamite AND cats!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh why you little!

jokes.text: Smithers... release the robotic Richard Simmons.
jokes.setup: Burns needing to respond to Homer's challenge
jokes.punchline: Has something even weirder than bee dogs
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns response - has something even more absurd than Homer imagined. Robotic Richard Simmons is inherently terrifying and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Bee dogs? That's nothing. I've got something far more sinister: a division of my factory devoted entirely to training attack llamas in the art of competitive ballroom dancing.

jokes.text: Come on, big boy! Shake the butter off those buns!
jokes.setup: Robotic fitness instructor attacking Homer
jokes.punchline: Delivering classic Richard Simmons motivational lines
jokes.explanation: Good character parody. The juxtaposition of motivational fitness speak with robotic menace creates uncomfortable comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Robotic Richard Simmons
dialogue.line: You can do it! Feel the burn! Come on, sweetie, push through the pain! I believe in you! You're a beautiful butterfly emerging from your cocoon of laziness and chardonnay!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ow! Ow! Ow!

jokes.text: His ass is gonna blow!
jokes.setup: Robotic Richard Simmons malfunctioning
jokes.punchline: Smithers's panicked warning about explosion
jokes.explanation: Simple escalation joke. The crude language from usually proper Smithers adds to the chaos, but it's not particularly sophisticated.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: He's going to explode!

jokes.text: The one who shot me was- Waylon Smithers! - No-o! Wait a minute. Yes.
jokes.setup: Big reveal of who shot Mr. Burns
jokes.punchline: Smithers's confused reaction and immediate confession
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Smithers - his loyalty conflicts with truth. The hesitation and admission shows his honesty despite devotion.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: It was me, sir! I shot you!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: You?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Yes, sir. I was so distraught over your affair with Lola Bunny that I came to the office that night, and in a fit of passion, I shot you. But... but I thought you were dead, sir. You mean you've been alive all this time?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Yes, you idiot.

jokes.text: So I wanted to kick up my heels and indulge my sweet tooth.
jokes.setup: Burns explaining his evil plan's success
jokes.punchline: Describes world domination in terms of mild self-indulgence
jokes.explanation: Good Burns characterization - treating megalomaniacal success like a minor personal reward. Shows his disconnect from normal human emotions.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Yes, my plan for world domination is proceeding splendidly. Soon the entire planet will be under my control, and I shall use this power to... get better lunch reservations.

jokes.text: drunk as a lemur
jokes.setup: Burns describing Smithers's condition
jokes.punchline: Unusual animal comparison for drunkenness
jokes.explanation: Creative simile that's both unexpected and oddly fitting. Burns would use an unusual animal comparison, making it good character work.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, you're drunk!

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: No sir, I'm just a little tired.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Tired? You can barely stand! You're swaying like a buoy in a hurricane.

jokes.text: I'm giving you a five percent pay cut. - Ohh!
jokes.setup: Burns punishing Smithers for attempted murder
jokes.punchline: Hilariously mild punishment for serious crime
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy - Burns treats attempted murder like a minor workplace infraction. Smithers's horrified reaction to the pay cut is equally perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, you attempted to murder me. For this heinous crime, I'm forced to dock your pay... ten cents per week.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Ten cents, sir? But sir, that's only thirty dollars a year!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Yes, and I expect you to make up the difference by working weekends. Unpaid, of course.

jokes.text: But, of course, for that ending to work... you would have to ignore all the Simpson D.N.A. evidence. And that would be downright nutty.
jokes.setup: Presenting alternate ending as if it could be real
jokes.punchline: Acknowledging it makes no sense while calling logic 'nutty'
jokes.explanation: Good meta-commentary acknowledging plot holes in their own fake ending. Troy's dismissive tone about logic is appropriately absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: Of course, this ending makes no sense whatsoever.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: But then again, what doesn't these days? You might say the laws of logic are just too... nutty.

jokes.text: since an old drunk made humans out of his rabbit characters... to pay off his gambling debts
jokes.setup: Reflecting on how far the Simpsons have come
jokes.punchline: Completely fictional dark origin story for Matt Groening
jokes.explanation: Dark, absurd alternate history for the show's creation. The specificity of 'gambling debts' and 'rabbit characters' makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You may remember me from such reflective moments as 'The Simpsons: A History' and 'Looking Back at Looking Back.' Well, did you know that Matt Groening's original concept for the show was much darker? In fact, the Simpsons were originally supposed to be a cannibalistic family living in a post-apocalyptic wasteland, and Homer was meant to be a sentient blob of nuclear waste. Marge's hair was actually a separate parasitic organism, and Bart was going to be 47 years old. Lisa would communicate only through saxophone solos played in morse code. It's true! I read it on the internet, which as we all know, is never wrong!

jokes.text: hard-core nudity!
jokes.setup: Troy promising what viewers came to see
jokes.punchline: Completely inappropriate content promise
jokes.explanation: Simple misdirection joke playing on sleazy TV special tropes. Troy's shameless delivery fits his character but the joke itself is fairly basic.

dialogue.speaker: Troy McClure
dialogue.line: You came here to see me promise you completely inappropriate content, and boy, do I deliver!

tmdb_overview: Troy McClure hosts a behind-the-scenes look at the show, including clips of the series' beginnings as shorts on "The Tracy Ullman Show," Simpsons trivia questions, and unaired scenes from popular episodes.
insight: Season 7's meta celebration lands 54 jokes in 22 minutes—pure escalation-driven absurdism.
